summary:
Shah Abbas I ('the Great', r. 1587–1629 AD) was the most capable ruler of the [[event:2338|Safavid Empire]] — a reforming monarch who transformed a state on the verge of dissolution into the dominant power of the Middle East, restructuring the military, relocating the capital, and building Isfahan into one of the most beautiful cities in the world. Abbas inherited a Safavid state that had been weakened by decades of succession conflict and [[event:2513|Ottoman and Uzbek wars]]; he restored its power through ruthless consolidation, executing or blinding potential rivals (including several of his own sons), and replacing the unreliable Qizilbash tribal cavalry with a professional army of converted Georgian, Armenian, and Circassian slaves (ghulam) — a system modeled on the Ottoman janissaries that made the army loyal to the shah rather than to tribal leaders.

Abbas made Persian Isfahan ('Isfahan is half the world' — a contemporary proverb) into a showcase of Safavid civilization. The Naqsh-e Jahan Square, flanked by the Ali Qapu palace, the Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque, and the Shah Mosque, remains one of the world's greatest urban ensembles and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Abbas welcomed European diplomats and merchants — including the English Shirley brothers who helped reorganize his military — using European connections to balance Ottoman pressure. His military reforms paid dividends: he recaptured Tabriz and Mesopotamia from the Ottomans (including Baghdad in 1624) and expelled the Portuguese from Hormuz (1622) with English naval assistance. The [[event:3632|Safavid Isfahan]] that Abbas created became a cultural center whose carpets, ceramics, and architectural innovations influenced Islamic art worldwide.
